sentences.text: Good morning dear visitors. Welcome to Rome City Tour. I'm your guide and my name is Alessandro.
I love Rome, this magic and eternal city. My family immigrated here from Albania about 30
years ago, but I consider myself a true Italian, because I was actually born here and have lived
here since then. During today's tour I'll share with you some fascinating facts about Rome itself,
the Vatican City and the Colosseum. Tomorrow,
we'll have a walk through the city and the city of Rome.
A walking tour to the site I love most, the Forum, the social, religious and political
center of the Roman Empire. Let's begin with some exciting facts about the city of Rome.
The city was founded by the brothers Romulus and Remus in 753 BC, which is considered the
official date of its foundation. However, excavations carried out in 2014 revealed
a wall built long before 753 BC. Thus it became clear that the city is much older
than it was in the early 20th century. The city is a city of the Roman Empire,
and it is stated in the official documents. In Rome, archaeologists uncovered a stone wall and
pieces of antique pottery dating back to the 9th century BC. The name of Rome is still associated
with the twin brothers, who according to a beautiful legend, were the sons of a human
mother and the god of war Mars, and were raised by a she-wolf. Rome is often referred to as the
cradle of Western civilization and Christian culture. Its historic center is listed as a
World Heritage Site. The city is a place of great importance to the Roman Empire, and is a place of
great importance to the Roman Empire. In total, there are 23 World Heritage Sites in Rome and
there are 59 of them in the whole Italy, making it the country with the most World Heritage Sites.
According to the most recent survey, Rome is the 14th most visited city in the world,
with 8.6 million tourists annually and the 4th most visited city in Europe following London,
Paris and Istanbul. As we approach the Colosseum, it's important to know that it was the largest
arena in the Roman Empire. The city was the largest arena in the Roman Empire, and it was
built in the year 1350. Construction of the Colosseum began under the Roman Emperor Vespasian
in about 70 AD. The Colosseum had 80 entrances and could accommodate between 50,000 to 80,000
spectators, the exact number remains unknown. We'll take a brief walk inside the Colosseum and
gain exclusive access to its huge arena. You probably think that this arena was used only
for gladiators' fights. This is not so. The huge arena of the Colosseum also hosted
public entertainment events, such as animal hunts and spectacular festivals based on Roman mythology,
which could last up to 100 days. Now it's time to visit the Vatican City.
The Vatican City State is the smallest country in the world in terms of both size and population.
It's only one-eighth the size of Central Park in New York City. The Vatican City houses world-famous
cultural sites, including St. Peter's Basilica and the Vatican Museums,
which contain 70,000 works of art with only 20,000 on display. The art collection includes
masterpieces by Raphael, Giotto, Caravaggio and other Renaissance geniuses. We'll explore the
galleries and admire the masterpieces by Michelangelo within the world-famous Sistine
Chapel. You'll also have the chance to see a painting by Leonardo da Vinci. By the way,
there is only one original painting by Leonardo in Rome and it's on display in
the Vatican Museums. If you wish to explore other parts of Rome, don't forget to use Google Maps
not to get lost in the historic labyrinths of this magnificent city. Good morning, dear visitors.
Welcome to Rome City Tour. I'm your guide and my name is Alessandro. I love Rome,
this magic and eternal city. My family immigrated here from Albania about 30 years ago,
but I consider myself a true Italian because I was actually born here and have lived here since then.
During today's tour I'll share with you some fascinating facts about Rome itself,
